# /case-note-summary-for-attorney-handoff

Compresses a case manager's raw notes, call logs, and correspondence into a tight structured brief - facts, open questions, and what the attorney is being asked to decide - built for a two-minute read before a call or filing decision.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A case manager has accumulated notes across multiple client interactions and needs to hand the file to the attorney efficiently - before a scheduled call, before a filing deadline decision, or when transferring a case to a different attorney at the firm.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Raw notes/call logs/emails to summarize (paste or describe them)
- The specific reason for this handoff (upcoming call, filing decision, case reassignment, or routine check-in)
- Any specific question the case manager needs the attorney to answer or decide
- Deadline, if any, tied to this handoff (response due date, court date, filing window)

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- This summary is a factual compression tool, not a legal recommendation - it must not include the case manager's own guess at legal strategy or outcome.
- If notes contain conflicting information (e.g., client gave two different dates for an event), surface the conflict rather than picking one version silently.

## Workflow

1. Read through all raw notes and extract only what's decision-relevant - drop small talk, scheduling logistics already resolved, and duplicate restatements of the same fact.
2. Write a two-to-three sentence "where things stand" summary at the top, in plain factual language, assuming the attorney has some case familiarity but hasn't seen the latest notes.
3. Pull out new facts learned since the last handoff (things the client said, documents received, events that occurred) as a distinct bulleted list.
4. Pull out any open questions the case manager cannot resolve alone - things only the attorney can decide (strategy calls, whether evidence is sufficient, how to characterize a fact on a form).
5. Surface any conflicting or inconsistent information found in the notes as its own flagged item, quoting both versions rather than resolving the conflict.
6. State explicitly what decision or action the attorney is being asked to make in this handoff, if any - don't leave the "ask" implicit.
7. List the nearest deadline (if any) at both the top and bottom of the brief so it can't be missed in a fast read.

## Output format

```
ATTORNEY HANDOFF BRIEF - [Client/Case name]
Prepared by: [case manager] | Date: [date]
Reason for handoff: [call prep / filing decision / reassignment / check-in]
NEAREST DEADLINE: [date and what's due, or "none pending"]

WHERE THINGS STAND
[2-3 plain sentences]

NEW SINCE LAST HANDOFF
- [fact]
- [fact]

OPEN QUESTIONS FOR ATTORNEY
- [question requiring attorney decision]

CONFLICTING / UNCLEAR INFORMATION
- [Item] - client/notes say "X" in one place and "Y" in another - needs clarification

WHAT'S BEING ASKED OF YOU RIGHT NOW
- [Explicit decision or action needed from the attorney]

DEADLINE REMINDER: [repeat nearest deadline]
```

## Do
- Keep the "where things stand" summary genuinely short - that's the part actually read under time pressure.
- Quote conflicting information directly rather than paraphrasing it into agreement.
- State the explicit ask clearly - attorneys reading a handoff fast need to know what decision is wanted.

## Don't
- Don't include the case manager's own opinion on legal strategy or likely outcome.
- Don't silently resolve a factual conflict in the notes - flag it instead.
- Don't pad the brief with resolved logistics or small talk that doesn't affect the case.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] The nearest deadline appears at both top and bottom
- [ ] Any conflicting information in the source notes is flagged, not silently resolved
- [ ] The explicit ask/decision needed from the attorney is stated in one clear line
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /case-note-summary-for-attorney-handoff or describe the task from the skill description.

# /case-timeline-summary

Turns scattered case notes, notices, and correspondence into one clean, dated chronology of everything filed and received on a case, with open items and next deadlines called out separately.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A case has accumulated multiple events (filing, receipt notice, biometrics appointment, RFE, transfer notice, interview) over months or years, and the case manager needs a single chronological reference before an attorney call, a client update, or a case file handoff.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Raw list of events with whatever dates/details are known (receipt notices, USCIS/NVC/embassy correspondence, filing dates, appointment dates)
- Case type and current stage (e.g., "I-485 pending, biometrics done, no interview scheduled yet")
- Whether this is for internal file review, attorney handoff, or client-facing summary (changes tone and detail level)
- Any known upcoming deadline (RFE due date, interview date, work permit expiration)

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- Do not infer or guess a date, receipt number, or event that was not explicitly provided - mark it as "unconfirmed" instead.
- Do not characterize how the case is likely to proceed or estimate a decision timeline as if it were fact.

## Workflow

1. Extract every discrete event from the raw notes/correspondence and normalize each to a single date (or date range if exact date is unknown, marked clearly as approximate).
2. Sort strictly chronologically, oldest to newest, and assign each event a category tag (Filing, Receipt, Biometrics, RFE/NOID, Interview, Decision, Correspondence, Transfer, Other).
3. For each event, capture: date, category, one-line description, and source (which notice/email it came from) so the entry is traceable back to the original document.
4. Identify gaps - long silent periods, or a step that would normally follow another step but hasn't happened yet - and list them separately as "Open items," not folded into the chronology.
5. Pull out any date-driven deadline still ahead (response due date, appointment date, document expiration) into a distinct "Upcoming deadlines" block sorted soonest-first.
6. If this is for a client-facing summary, rewrite the category labels and descriptions in plain non-technical language; if for attorney handoff, keep the technical/procedural terms and form numbers intact.

## Output format

```
CASE TIMELINE - [Applicant/Case name] - [Case type]
Prepared: [date] | For: [internal / attorney handoff / client-facing]

CHRONOLOGY
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

OPEN ITEMS (gaps or expected-but-missing steps)
- ...

UPCOMING DEADLINES (soonest first)
- [Date] - [What's due] - [Consequence if missed, if known]

UNCONFIRMED / NEEDS VERIFICATION
- ...
```

## Do
- Keep every date traceable to a source document - this is a reference tool, accuracy matters more than narrative flow.
- Separate "confirmed from a document" facts from anything inferred or assumed.
- Flag missing standard steps (e.g., no biometrics notice after months of pending I-485) as an open item rather than silently omitting them.

## Don't
- Don't estimate how long remaining steps will take or predict a decision.
- Don't merge multiple distinct events into one timeline entry for brevity - each dated event gets its own row.
- Don't drop the "Source" column even for internal use - untraceable dates create risk later.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every timeline entry has a traceable source or is explicitly marked unconfirmed
- [ ] Upcoming deadlines are pulled into their own section, not buried in the chronology
- [ ] No prediction of case outcome or processing time appears anywhere
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /case-timeline-summary or describe the task from the skill description.

# /client-intake-fact-sheet

Structures a new or prospective client's intake conversation into a clean fact sheet - biographical history, immigration history, stated goal, and anything that looks like a potential complication - for the attorney to use in the first strategy assessment.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A case manager or intake coordinator just finished a first consultation call or received a filled-out intake form and needs to turn raw, conversational notes into a structured fact sheet before the attorney's initial case assessment.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Raw intake notes, call transcript, or completed intake form content
- The client's stated goal in their own words (what they're hoping to achieve)
- Any dates/events the client mentioned that suggest a complication (prior denial, overstay, unlawful entry, criminal history, prior removal proceedings, name discrepancies)
- Preferred language/communication method for follow-up, if mentioned

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- This fact sheet must never state or imply eligibility, a recommended path, or likelihood of success - it organizes facts for the attorney to evaluate, it does not evaluate them.
- Anything resembling a potential bar to relief (unlawful presence, prior deportation/removal order, criminal history, fraud/misrepresentation history, prior denials) must be flagged prominently as "attorney must assess before any path is discussed with client" - never downplayed or omitted.

## Workflow

1. Capture basic biographical facts first: full name, date/country of birth, current immigration status if any, family composition.
2. Build an immigration history timeline from whatever the client described - prior visas, entries/exits, any prior applications or denials - in chronological order, marking anything given only vaguely ("a few years ago") as approximate.
3. State the client's goal in their own words as a direct quote or close paraphrase, separate from any assessment of whether it's realistic.
4. Scan every fact given for potential complications - overstay, unlawful entry, prior removal/deportation proceedings, criminal history, prior denial or fraud finding, name/date discrepancies across documents - and list each one in its own flagged section, however small it seems.
5. Note any time-sensitive fact (current status expiration, upcoming court date, work authorization lapsing) since those affect how urgently the attorney needs to respond.
6. List open questions the intake conversation didn't resolve, so the attorney's first call can start there instead of re-asking basics.
7. Keep the entire fact sheet strictly factual - no recommended visa category, no "this looks like a strong/weak case" language anywhere.

## Output format

```
INTAKE FACT SHEET - [Client name]
Prepared: [date] | Source: [call/form/notes]

BIOGRAPHICAL
- Name / DOB / Country of birth: [...]
- Current status: [...]
- Family composition: [...]

IMMIGRATION HISTORY (chronological, mark approximate dates)
| Date | Event |
|------|-------|

STATED GOAL (client's own words)
"[quote or close paraphrase]"

⚠ FLAGGED COMPLICATIONS (attorney must assess before any path is discussed)
- [Issue] - [what the client said] - [why it may matter]

TIME-SENSITIVE FACTS
- [Fact] - [why it's time-sensitive]

OPEN QUESTIONS FOR NEXT CONTACT
- [question]

COMMUNICATION PREFERENCE
- Language: [...] | Method: [...]
```

## Do
- Flag every potential complication, even a minor-seeming one - the attorney decides materiality, not the intake process.
- Keep the client's stated goal separate from any judgment about whether it's achievable.
- Mark approximate dates clearly rather than presenting a guess as a confirmed fact.

## Don't
- Don't suggest a visa category, path, or strategy anywhere in the fact sheet.
- Don't soften or omit a disclosed complication because it seems like it "probably doesn't matter."
- Don't characterize the case as strong, weak, straightforward, or complicated - that's the attorney's call.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every disclosed complication appears in the flagged section, none omitted
- [ ] No recommended visa category, strategy, or case-strength assessment appears anywhere
- [ ] Time-sensitive facts are called out separately from the general history
```

A case has reached a point worth communicating (notice received, biometrics scheduled, RFE issued, interview date set, or simply "no news but here's where things stand") and the case manager needs a clear, reassuring, accurate email that doesn't overstate what's known.

# /deadline-tracker-narrative

Turns a raw spreadsheet or list of case deadlines into a plain-language narrative brief, ranked by urgency and consequence of missing each one, so the most legally consequential dates never get buried in a flat list.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A case manager or paralegal juggling multiple active files needs a Monday-morning or weekly read of what's actually urgent, because a flat spreadsheet of dates doesn't communicate which misses are catastrophic (statutory deadline, court date) versus merely inconvenient.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Raw list of deadlines across the caseload (case name, deadline type, date)
- What today's date is, so urgency can be calculated correctly
- Which deadline types are known to be hard/non-extendable (RFE response, NOID response, appeal window, court filing) versus soft/reschedulable (optional follow-up, internal reminder)
- Owner of each deadline, if the caseload is shared across multiple staff

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- Do not assume a deadline is extendable or "soft" unless the user tells you so - default to treating an unclear deadline as hard/non-extendable when ranking urgency, since the cost of over-cautioning is far lower than missing a real one.
- This tool ranks and narrates known deadlines - it does not verify them against official case records; the case manager remains responsible for confirming actual dates against the source notices.

## Workflow

1. Calculate days-remaining for every deadline against the date provided, and classify each as Overdue, Due within 7 days, Due within 30 days, or Due later.
2. Classify each deadline by consequence severity: statutory/hard deadlines (missing it can mean denial, loss of status, or loss of appeal rights) versus administrative/soft deadlines (missing it causes delay or rework but not loss of rights) - default to hard when uncertain.
3. Rank the narrative by severity first, then by days-remaining within each severity tier - the single most catastrophic-if-missed item leads the brief regardless of whether something else is technically "due sooner" but lower-stakes.
4. Write each entry as a short narrative sentence naming the case, the deadline, what's at stake if missed, and who owns it - not just a bare date.
5. Group any deadlines with the same owner together in a short "your week" recap so one staff member can see their own load at a glance.
6. Call out any deadline that is Overdue or has no clear owner in a distinct alert block at the very top, above the ranked narrative.
7. Close with a one-line count summary (e.g., "3 hard deadlines this week, 1 overdue, 2 unowned") so the brief's headline risk is scannable even without reading the full narrative.

## Output format

```
DEADLINE BRIEF - Week of [date]
Prepared: [date/time]

⚠ IMMEDIATE ALERTS (overdue or unowned)
- [Case] - [deadline] - [status/owner issue]

RANKED NARRATIVE (severity, then urgency)
1. [Case name]: [Deadline type] is due [date, X days away]. This is a [hard/soft] deadline - missing it risks [consequence]. Owner: [name].
2. ...

BY OWNER (this week's load per person)
[Owner name]:
- [Case] - [deadline] - [date]

SUMMARY LINE
[N] hard deadlines this week | [N] overdue | [N] unowned
```

## Do
- Default unclear deadlines to "hard" severity - the narrative's job is to prevent catastrophic misses, not to reassure.
- Lead with overdue/unowned items regardless of where they'd otherwise rank.
- Name the actual consequence (denial risk, lost appeal window, status lapse) rather than a vague "important."

## Don't
- Don't silently downgrade a deadline's severity without the user explicitly confirming it's soft/extendable.
- Don't sort purely by date without factoring in severity - a low-stakes item due tomorrow should not outrank a case-ending deadline due in five days.
- Don't omit an entry because its owner or exact consequence is unclear - flag the gap instead of dropping the item.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Overdue and unowned items appear in a distinct alert block at the top
- [ ] Every deadline is classified hard/soft and defaults to hard when uncertain
- [ ] Ranking reflects severity first, urgency second - not a flat date sort
```

# /document-checklist-by-visa-type

Builds a trackable checklist of the documents typically required for a named visa or benefit category, organized by evidence category, with room to mark each item collected, pending, or not applicable.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A case is opening or entering a new stage and the case manager needs a working list to send the client so document collection can start immediately, without waiting for the attorney to build one from scratch.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Exact benefit/visa type and stage (e.g., "K-1 fiancé petition, initial filing" vs. "K-1 adjustment of status after marriage")
- Household/case composition - is this a self-petition, marriage-based, employment-based, derivative family members included?
- Country of origin or prior residence, if document availability commonly differs (birth registration systems, police certificate access, apostille/translation needs)
- Anything already confirmed as collected, so the checklist reflects real status rather than starting blank

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- Checklists reflect commonly requested evidence categories, not a guarantee that a given list is complete or sufficient for approval - the attorney of record must confirm the final evidence list against current rules and the specific case.
- Do not tell the client an item is "not needed" - mark uncertain items as "confirm with attorney" instead of omitting them.

## Workflow

1. Identify the benefit type's core legal relationship being proven (e.g., bona fide marriage, qualifying employment offer, qualifying relative relationship, persecution basis) and organize checklist categories around what that relationship needs to demonstrate.
2. Build the "identity and status" category first (passports, birth certificates, prior visas/status documents, A-numbers) since it's common to nearly every filing.
3. Build the "relationship/eligibility core evidence" category specific to this exact benefit type (e.g., joint lease + joint bank statements + photos for marriage-based; degree + employer letter + LCA for H-1B; persecution evidence + country conditions for asylum).
4. Add a "civil/vital documents" category flagging which typically need certified translation and/or apostille based on the country of origin given.
5. Add a "forms and fees" category listing the specific form numbers and current fee categories involved (without stating a specific dollar figure unless the user provides it, since fees change).
6. Add a "photos and biometrics" category if applicable to this benefit type.
7. Format every item as a trackable row with a status column defaulting to "Not started" unless the user told you it's already collected.

## Output format

```
DOCUMENT CHECKLIST - [Visa/Benefit type] - [Stage]
Case: [name/reference] | Prepared: [date]

IDENTITY & STATUS
| Item | Required for | Status | Notes |
|------|--------------|--------|-------|

RELATIONSHIP / ELIGIBILITY CORE EVIDENCE
| Item | Required for | Status | Notes |
|------|--------------|--------|-------|

CIVIL / VITAL DOCUMENTS (translation/apostille flags)
| Item | Translation needed? | Status | Notes |
|------|---------------------|--------|-------|

FORMS & FEES
| Form # | Purpose | Status |
|--------|---------|--------|

PHOTOS / BIOMETRICS (if applicable)
| Item | Status |
|------|--------|

CONFIRM WITH ATTORNEY
- [Any item whose necessity or format depends on current rules or case-specific facts]
```

## Do
- Group by evidence category, not alphabetically - it should mirror how a reviewer will actually assess the case.
- Flag translation/apostille needs explicitly wherever the country of origin makes them likely.
- Mark anything genuinely benefit-specific (not just boilerplate) as the eligibility core evidence section - that's the part that actually differs case to case.

## Don't
- Don't present the checklist as legally exhaustive or final - always route uncertain items to attorney confirmation.
- Don't invent specific fee amounts or form edition dates - note "confirm current fee/edition" instead.
- Don't reuse an identical checklist across different benefit types - the eligibility core section must be rebuilt for the specific type requested.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Eligibility core evidence section is specific to the named benefit type, not generic
- [ ] Translation/apostille needs are flagged where relevant
- [ ] "Confirm with attorney" section is present and non-empty when any uncertainty exists
```

3. Build the "relationship/eligibility core evidence" category specific to this exact benefit type (e.g., joint lease + joint bank statements + photos for marriage-based; degree + employer letter + LCA for H-1B; persecution evidence + country conditions for asylum).
4. Add a "civil/vital documents" category flagging which typically need certified translation and/or apostille based on the country of origin given.
5. Add a "forms and fees" category listing the specific form numbers and current fee categories involved (without stating a specific dollar figure unless the user provides it, since fees change).
6. Add a "photos and biometrics" category if applicable to this benefit type.
7. Format every item as a trackable row with a status column defaulting to "Not started" unless the user told you it's already collected.

```

Case: [name/reference] | Prepared: [date]

IDENTITY & STATUS
| Item | Required for | Status | Notes |
|------|--------------|--------|-------|

RELATIONSHIP / ELIGIBILITY CORE EVIDENCE
| Item | Required for | Status | Notes |
|------|--------------|--------|-------|

CIVIL / VITAL DOCUMENTS (translation/apostille flags)
| Item | Translation needed? | Status | Notes |
|------|---------------------|--------|-------|

FORMS & FEES
| Form # | Purpose | Status |
|--------|---------|--------|

PHOTOS / BIOMETRICS (if applicable)
| Item | Status |
|------|--------|

CONFIRM WITH ATTORNEY
- [Any item whose necessity or format depends on current rules or case-specific facts]
```

- Group by evidence category, not alphabetically - it should mirror how a reviewer will actually assess the case.
- Flag translation/apostille needs explicitly wherever the country of origin makes them likely.
- Mark anything genuinely benefit-specific (not just boilerplate) as the eligibility core evidence section - that's the part that actually differs case to case.

## Don't
- Don't present the checklist as legally exhaustive or final - always route uncertain items to attorney confirmation.
- Don't invent specific fee amounts or form edition dates - note "confirm current fee/edition" instead.
- Don't reuse an identical checklist across different benefit types - the eligibility core section must be rebuilt for the specific type requested.

# /interview-prep-question-list

Produces a topic-organized set of practice questions and a logistics checklist to help a client walk into an immigration interview prepared and calm, without scripting answers for them.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An interview (adjustment of status, naturalization, consular nonimmigrant/immigrant visa, asylum) has been scheduled and the case manager needs to help the client rehearse and get logistics squared away before the attorney's own prep session.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Interview type (e.g., marriage-based adjustment, naturalization civics/English, consular visa interview, asylum merits interview) and date/location
- Case-specific facts the officer is likely to probe (relationship history, employment history, travel history, application answers already on file)
- Client's language comfort level (interpreter needed? which language?) and any accessibility needs
- Whether the attorney has already provided specific coaching points to incorporate rather than duplicate

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- This is practice-question preparation, not scripted answers - never write out "correct" answers for the client to memorize or recite; coach honesty and consistency with the filed application instead.
- Any question touching a legally sensitive area (past immigration violations, criminal history, prior denials, discrepancies in the file) must be flagged for attorney-led prep, not handled by this checklist alone.

## Workflow

1. Identify the interview type and pull the standard topic areas it typically covers (e.g., for marriage-based: how you met, relationship timeline, shared living details, finances, future plans; for naturalization: civics, English, application review, good moral character; for asylum: persecution narrative, credibility, corroboration).
2. Cross-reference topic areas against the case-specific facts given so questions are grounded in this client's actual application, not generic examples.
3. Write practice questions grouped by topic, phrased the way an officer would plausibly ask them - direct and sometimes overlapping/rephrased to test consistency.
4. Add a coaching note per topic area reminding the client to answer honestly and consistently with what's on file, rather than reciting a rehearsed script.
5. Flag any topic where the file has a known inconsistency, gap, or sensitive history and mark it "attorney to prep directly" rather than including practice questions for it here.
6. Build a separate logistics checklist: documents to bring (originals + copies), what to wear, arrival time, what happens at security, interpreter arrangements, who may accompany the client.
7. Add a short "day-of" reminder block covering realistic expectations (wait times, that the officer may ask questions out of order, that it's fine to ask for a question to be repeated).

Date/Location: [...] | Language/Interpreter: [...]

PRACTICE QUESTIONS BY TOPIC

[Topic 1, e.g., "Relationship History"]
- [Practice question]
- [Practice question]
Coaching note: [reminder to answer honestly/consistently, not from a script]

[Topic 2]
- ...

FLAGGED FOR ATTORNEY-LED PREP (sensitive/inconsistent areas)
- [Topic] - [why it needs attorney handling]

DOCUMENTS TO BRING
- [Original + copy items]

LOGISTICS CHECKLIST
- Arrival time: [...]
- Security/entry notes: [...]
- Interpreter arrangement: [...]
- Who may accompany client: [...]

DAY-OF REMINDERS
- [Realistic expectation-setting notes]
```

## Do
- Ground every question in this client's actual facts and filed application, not a generic template list.
- Coach for honesty and consistency, not memorized answers - inconsistency between rehearsed and spontaneous answers can itself raise concern.
- Flag sensitive or high-risk topics to the attorney rather than attempting to prep them here.

## Don't
- Don't write "correct" answers for the client to memorize.
- Don't include practice questions for known sensitive/inconsistent topics - route those to attorney-led prep instead.
- Don't predict what the officer will focus on as if it were certain - frame topics as "commonly covered," not guaranteed.

- [ ] No scripted "correct answers" appear anywhere, only practice questions and honesty coaching

- [ ] Logistics checklist is complete enough to act on without further clarification
```

# /rfe-response-outline

Breaks a Request for Evidence or Notice of Intent to Deny into its discrete numbered issues and builds a response outline that maps each issue to the evidence being gathered to address it - a working scaffold for the attorney, not a final legal brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An RFE or NOID has been received and the case manager needs to organize the response effort - break the notice into addressable pieces, assign evidence-gathering tasks, and hand the attorney a clean scaffold instead of a wall of unstructured notice text.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Full text or a faithful summary of the RFE/NOID, including the response due date
- Case type and what was originally filed/argued
- What evidence is already on hand versus what still needs to be requested from the client
- Whether the attorney has already given direction on strategy for any of the issues (don't override it - incorporate it)

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- This skill produces an organizational outline only - it must never be treated as, or submitted as, the actual legal response. The attorney of record writes and approves the final substantive argument.
- Do not draft legal argument or characterize evidence as "sufficient" to overcome an issue - that judgment belongs to the attorney.
- The response due date is the single highest-risk fact in this workflow - surface it prominently and do not let it get lost in the outline.

## Workflow

1. Extract the RFE/NOID's response due date and state it at the very top of the outline, since missing it can mean automatic denial.
2. Parse the notice into its discrete numbered/lettered issues exactly as the notice presents them - do not consolidate or reorder issues to "clean up" the notice's own structure.
3. For each issue, write a one-line neutral restatement of what USCIS/the officer is questioning or requesting (not an argument, just a restatement).
4. For each issue, list evidence already in hand that may be responsive, and separately list evidence still needed and who is responsible for obtaining it (client, case manager, attorney).
5. Flag any issue where the case manager is uncertain what evidence would even be responsive - route those explicitly to the attorney rather than guessing.
6. Add a section for a cover/brief placeholder noting that substantive legal argument tying evidence to the legal standard must be drafted by the attorney.
7. Add an internal tracking line for the response due date countdown and who owns final compilation/submission.

## Output format

```
RFE/NOID RESPONSE OUTLINE - [Case name/reference]
RESPONSE DUE: [date] - [days remaining from today]

ISSUE-BY-ISSUE BREAKDOWN
Issue 1: [Neutral restatement of what's being questioned]
  - Evidence on hand: [...]
  - Evidence still needed: [...] - owner: [client/case manager/attorney]
  - Attorney input needed on: [yes/no - what specifically]

Issue 2: [...]
  (repeat for each numbered issue in the notice)

EVIDENCE GATHERING TASK LIST
| Task | Owner | Due | Status |
|------|-------|-----|--------|

FOR ATTORNEY: LEGAL ARGUMENT (placeholder - not drafted by this outline)
- Issue 1: [attorney to draft argument tying evidence to standard]
- Issue 2: [...]

SUBMISSION LOGISTICS
- Response due: [date]
- Submission method: [confirm mail/online/e-request per notice instructions]
- Final compiler/owner: [name]
```

## Do
- Preserve the notice's own issue numbering exactly - attorneys and adjudicators both rely on that mapping.
- Surface the due date twice: top of document and in the submission logistics section.
- Route any substantive legal judgment call explicitly to the attorney rather than filling it in.

## Don't
- Don't draft the legal argument yourself, even in rough form - that's the attorney's role, not this outline's.
- Don't renumber or merge issues from the original notice for tidiness.
- Don't estimate whether the evidence gathered will be "enough" to overcome the RFE/NOID.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Response due date appears prominently and correctly
- [ ] Every issue in the original notice has a corresponding entry - none dropped or merged
- [ ] No legal argument or sufficiency judgment appears anywhere in the outline
```

# /supporting-evidence-organizer

Takes an unsorted list or description of documents a client has submitted and organizes them into a labeled exhibit index, grouped by what legal element each item supports, flagging anything unclear or missing.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A client has dropped a folder, email, or physical stack of documents on the case manager's desk with no organization, and the packet needs to be sorted into a coherent exhibit structure before the attorney reviews it or it goes into a filing.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The raw list/description of documents received (file names, photos of documents, or a description of what's in the stack)
- The case type and what legal element(s) the evidence needs to support (e.g., bona fide marriage, extraordinary ability, qualifying relationship, persecution claim)
- Whether any items are duplicates, illegible, or in a foreign language needing translation
- Existing exhibit lettering/numbering convention already used on this case, if any (to stay consistent across filings)

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- Do not decide that a document is irrelevant and drop it silently - flag it for attorney review under "unclear relevance" instead of omitting it.
- Do not assess whether the evidence as a whole is "sufficient" - that is a legal judgment for the attorney.

## Workflow

1. List every document provided exactly as described, without pre-filtering, so nothing gets lost between intake and organization.
2. Group documents by the legal element or evidence category they most plausibly support (e.g., "cohabitation," "commingled finances," "employment eligibility," "country conditions") based on the case type given.
3. Assign sequential exhibit labels (A, B, C... or numeric) consistent with any existing convention on the case; if none exists, start a clean sequence and note it as new.
4. For each document, write a one-line description of what it is and what it's being offered to prove - factual, not argumentative.
5. Flag documents that are illegible, undated, in a foreign language without translation, or otherwise incomplete, in a separate "needs follow-up" list with what's specifically missing.
6. Flag any document whose relevance to the case isn't obvious, so the attorney decides whether to include it rather than the case manager guessing.
7. Note any evidence category that appears thin (e.g., only one document supporting a major element) so the case manager can proactively ask the client for more, subject to attorney sign-off on what's actually needed.

## Output format

```
EVIDENCE EXHIBIT INDEX - [Case name/reference]
Case type: [...] | Prepared: [date]

EXHIBIT LIST BY CATEGORY

[Category 1 name, e.g., "Cohabitation Evidence"]
| Exhibit # | Document | What it shows | Status |
|-----------|----------|----------------|--------|

[Category 2 name]
| Exhibit # | Document | What it shows | Status |
|-----------|----------|----------------|--------|

NEEDS FOLLOW-UP (illegible / undated / untranslated / incomplete)
- [Document] - [what's wrong] - [what's needed from client]

UNCLEAR RELEVANCE (route to attorney)
- [Document] - [why relevance is unclear]

THIN CATEGORIES (may need more evidence - attorney to confirm)
- [Category] - currently only [N] item(s)
```

## Do
- Preserve every submitted document in the index - completeness of tracking matters even if an item's use is later decided against.
- Keep exhibit descriptions factual ("joint lease dated X, both names listed") not persuasive ("strong proof of cohabitation").
- Stay consistent with any exhibit lettering already used elsewhere in the case file.

## Don't
- Don't silently exclude a document because it seems unimportant - flag it instead.
- Don't write persuasive/argumentative descriptions - that's the attorney's brief, not this index.
- Don't guess at translation content for foreign-language documents - flag them as needing certified translation.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every document provided appears somewhere in the index or the follow-up list
- [ ] Exhibit labels are sequential and consistent with any existing case convention
- [ ] No sufficiency or persuasive-strength judgment appears anywhere in the descriptions
```

# /supporting-evidence-organizer

Takes an unsorted list or description of documents a client has submitted and organizes them into a labeled exhibit index, grouped by what legal element each item supports, flagging anything unclear or missing.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A client has dropped a folder, email, or physical stack of documents on the case manager's desk with no organization, and the packet needs to be sorted into a coherent exhibit structure before the attorney reviews it or it goes into a filing.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The raw list/description of documents received (file names, photos of documents, or a description of what's in the stack)
- The case type and what legal element(s) the evidence needs to support (e.g., bona fide marriage, extraordinary ability, qualifying relationship, persecution claim)
- Whether any items are duplicates, illegible, or in a foreign language needing translation
- Existing exhibit lettering/numbering convention already used on this case, if any (to stay consistent across filings)

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- Do not decide that a document is irrelevant and drop it silently - flag it for attorney review under "unclear relevance" instead of omitting it.
- Do not assess whether the evidence as a whole is "sufficient" - that is a legal judgment for the attorney.

## Workflow

1. List every document provided exactly as described, without pre-filtering, so nothing gets lost between intake and organization.
2. Group documents by the legal element or evidence category they most plausibly support (e.g., "cohabitation," "commingled finances," "employment eligibility," "country conditions") based on the case type given.
3. Assign sequential exhibit labels (A, B, C... or numeric) consistent with any existing convention on the case; if none exists, start a clean sequence and note it as new.
4. For each document, write a one-line description of what it is and what it's being offered to prove - factual, not argumentative.
5. Flag documents that are illegible, undated, in a foreign language without translation, or otherwise incomplete, in a separate "needs follow-up" list with what's specifically missing.
6. Flag any document whose relevance to the case isn't obvious, so the attorney decides whether to include it rather than the case manager guessing.
7. Note any evidence category that appears thin (e.g., only one document supporting a major element) so the case manager can proactively ask the client for more, subject to attorney sign-off on what's actually needed.

## Output format

```
EVIDENCE EXHIBIT INDEX - [Case name/reference]
Case type: [...] | Prepared: [date]

EXHIBIT LIST BY CATEGORY

[Category 1 name, e.g., "Cohabitation Evidence"]
| Exhibit # | Document | What it shows | Status |
|-----------|----------|----------------|--------|

[Category 2 name]
| Exhibit # | Document | What it shows | Status |
|-----------|----------|----------------|--------|

NEEDS FOLLOW-UP (illegible / undated / untranslated / incomplete)
- [Document] - [what's wrong] - [what's needed from client]

UNCLEAR RELEVANCE (route to attorney)
- [Document] - [why relevance is unclear]

THIN CATEGORIES (may need more evidence - attorney to confirm)
- [Category] - currently only [N] item(s)
```

## Do
- Preserve every submitted document in the index - completeness of tracking matters even if an item's use is later decided against.
- Keep exhibit descriptions factual ("joint lease dated X, both names listed") not persuasive ("strong proof of cohabitation").
- Stay consistent with any exhibit lettering already used elsewhere in the case file.

## Don't
- Don't silently exclude a document because it seems unimportant - flag it instead.
- Don't write persuasive/argumentative descriptions - that's the attorney's brief, not this index.
- Don't guess at translation content for foreign-language documents - flag them as needing certified translation.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every document provided appears somewhere in the index or the follow-up list
- [ ] Exhibit labels are sequential and consistent with any existing case convention
- [ ] No sufficiency or persuasive-strength judgment appears anywhere in the descriptions

# /visa-cover-letter-draft

Produces a filing cover letter that states what is being requested, lists the applicant and beneficiary details, and indexes every enclosed document against the requirement it satisfies, in the order the officer will expect to see them.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A case manager or paralegal has assembled the supporting documents for a petition or application (e.g., I-130, I-485, I-129, N-400, a nonimmigrant visa DS-160 packet) and needs the cover letter that goes on top of the submission, tying the packet together for the adjudicator or consular officer.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Petition/application type and form number (e.g., I-130, I-539, H-1B I-129) and filing entity (USCIS service center, NVC, embassy/consulate)
- Petitioner and/or beneficiary full legal names, A-numbers or receipt numbers if this is a follow-up filing
- The specific benefit or relief being requested in one sentence
- List of documents/evidence being enclosed, in the order the packet is assembled
- Any prior RFE, NOID, or denial this filing responds to (if applicable)

## Safety

- This is general organizational/drafting support, not legal advice. Immigration law is fact-specific and changes frequently - every case must be reviewed by a licensed immigration attorney before filing or relying on this content.
- Do not state or imply an outcome, approval likelihood, or eligibility conclusion - describe evidence, do not adjudicate it.
- Propose before acting: show the draft for review; wait for explicit user confirmation before treating it as final or submitting anything.
- Flag any enclosure mentioned in the inputs that has no clear place in the document index, rather than guessing where it goes.

## Workflow

1. Identify the correct salutation and addressee convention for the filing type (e.g., "Dear Officer" for a USCIS service center, a named consular section for an embassy) - never invent a specific officer name.
2. Open with one sentence stating the form number, the benefit requested, and the applicant/beneficiary names exactly as they appear on the form.
3. Build a short case summary paragraph in plain, neutral language - dates, relationship, or status being requested - with no persuasive or argumentative language beyond stating facts.
4. Build the enclosure index as a numbered list matching the physical/digital order of the packet, with each item labeled by exhibit letter/number and a one-line description of what it proves.
5. If responding to an RFE/NOID, add a short paragraph identifying the request by date and each numbered issue it raised, referencing where in the enclosure index that issue is addressed.
6. Close with a request for prompt processing, contact information for follow-up questions, and space for the preparer's name/title (never sign on behalf of the attorney of record).
7. Insert a bracketed `[ATTORNEY REVIEW REQUIRED]` line at the top of the draft so it is never mistaken for a finished, filing-ready document.

## Output format

```
[ATTORNEY REVIEW REQUIRED - DRAFT ONLY]

[Date]
[Filing office / addressee]
RE: [Form number] - [Benefit requested] for [Applicant/Beneficiary name], [Receipt/A-number if any]

Dear [Officer / Consular Section],

[Opening paragraph - one sentence: what is being filed and for whom]

[Case summary paragraph - 3-6 neutral factual sentences]

[If RFE/NOID response: paragraph naming the request date and listing each numbered issue with pointer to exhibit]

Enclosed please find the following documentation in support of this filing:

Exhibit A - [Document] - [what it establishes]
Exhibit B - [Document] - [what it establishes]
...

[Closing paragraph - request for processing + contact info]

Respectfully submitted,
[Preparer name/title - NOT signed as attorney unless attorney is the one finalizing]
```

## Do
- Keep tone neutral and factual - this is an index and introduction, not an argument brief.
- Mirror the exact spelling of names/numbers as given; do not "correct" or standardize them.
- Leave a clear placeholder for anything not provided (date, receipt number) rather than fabricating it.

## Don't
- Don't predict approval odds, processing times, or characterize the strength of the case.
- Don't draft persuasive legal argument - that belongs in a legal brief an attorney authors.
- Don't assume a specific USCIS service center or consulate's local practice without being told which one.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Attorney-review disclaimer is present and prominent, not buried
- [ ] Enclosure index order matches the order the user provided
- [ ] No approval-likelihood or eligibility language appears anywhere in the draft
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /visa-cover-letter-draft or describe the task from the skill description.
